title: Cromwell
content:
聽聽聽聽They took dead Cromwell from his grave,
聽聽聽聽And stuck his head on high;
聽聽聽聽The Merry Monarch and his men,
聽聽聽聽They laughed as they passed by
聽聽聽聽The common people cheered and jeered,
聽聽聽聽To England锟絪 deep disgrace,
聽聽聽聽The crowds who锟絛 ne锟絜r have dared to look
聽聽聽聽Live Cromwell in the face.

聽聽聽聽He came in England锟絪 direst need
聽聽聽聽With law and fire and sword,
聽聽聽聽He thrashed her enemies at home
聽聽聽聽And crushed her foes abroad;
聽聽聽聽He kept his word by sea and land,
聽聽聽聽His parliament he schooled,
聽聽聽聽He made the nations understand
聽聽聽聽A Man in England ruled!

聽聽聽聽Van Tromp, with twice the English ships,
聽聽聽聽And flushed by victory,
聽聽聽聽A great broom to his masthead bound,
聽聽聽聽Set sail to sweep the sea.
聽聽聽聽But England锟絪 ruler was a man
聽聽聽聽Who needed lots of room,
聽聽聽聽So Blake soon lowered the Dutchman锟絪 tone,
聽聽聽聽And smashed the Dutchman锟絪 broom.

聽聽聽聽He sent a bill to Tuscany
聽聽聽聽For sixty thousand pounds,
聽聽聽聽For wrong done to his subjects there,
聽聽聽聽And merchants in her bounds.
聽聽聽聽He sent by Debt Collector Blake,
聽聽聽聽And, you need but be told
聽聽聽聽That, by the Duke of Tuscany
聽聽聽聽That bill was paid in gold.

聽聽聽聽To pirate ports in Africa
聽聽聽聽He sent a message grim
聽聽聽聽To have each captured Englishman
聽聽聽聽Delivered up to him;
聽聽聽聽And every ship and cargo锟絪 worth,
聽聽聽聽And every boat and gun,
聽聽聽聽And this, all this, as Dickens says,
聽聽聽聽锟絎as gloriously done.锟絓n
聽聽聽聽They锟絛 tortured English prisoners
聽聽聽聽Who锟絛 sailed the Spanish Main;
聽聽聽聽So Cromwell sent a little bill
聽聽聽聽By Admiral Blake to Spain.
聽聽聽聽To keep his hand in, by the way.
聽聽聽聽He whipped the Portuguese;
聽聽聽聽And he made it safe for English ships
聽聽聽聽To sail the Spanish seas.

聽聽聽聽The Protestants in Southern lands
聽聽聽聽Had long been sore oppressed;
聽聽聽聽They sent their earnest prayers to Noll
聽聽聽聽To have their wrongs redressed.
聽聽聽聽He sent a message to the Powers,
聽聽聽聽In which he told them flat,
聽聽聽聽All men must praise God as they chose,
聽聽聽聽Or he would see to that.

聽聽聽聽And, when he锟絛 hanged the fools at home
聽聽聽聽And settled foreign rows,
聽聽聽聽He found the time to potter round
聽聽聽聽Amongst his pigs and cows.
聽聽聽聽Of private rows he never spoke,
聽聽聽聽That grand old Ironsides.
聽聽聽聽They said a father锟絪 strong heart broke
聽聽聽聽When Cromwell锟絪 daughter died.

聽聽聽聽(They dragged his body from its grave,
聽聽聽聽His head stuck on a pole,
聽聽聽聽They threw his wife锟絪 and daughter锟絪 bones
聽聽聽聽Into a rubbish hole
聽聽聽聽To rot with those of two who锟絛 lived
聽聽聽聽And fought for England锟絪 sake,
聽聽聽聽And each one in his own brave way,
聽聽聽聽Great Pym, and Admiral Blake.)

聽聽聽聽From Charles to Charles, throughout the world
聽聽聽聽Old England锟絪 name was high,
聽聽聽聽And that锟絪 a thing no Royalist
聽聽聽聽Could ever yet deny.
聽聽聽聽Long shameful years have passed since then,
聽聽聽聽In spite of England锟絪 boast,
聽聽聽聽But Englishmen were Englishmen,
聽聽聽聽While Cromwell carved the roast.

聽聽聽聽And, in my country锟絪 hour of need,
聽聽聽聽For it shall surely come,
聽聽聽聽While run by fools who锟絣l never heed
聽聽聽聽The beating of the drum.
聽聽聽聽While baffled by the fools at home,
聽聽聽聽And threatened from the sea,
聽聽聽聽Lord! send a man like Oliver,
聽聽聽聽And let me live to see.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Henry Lawson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Henry Lawson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
